text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY JOINT SURVEILLANCE NOTE
12-Month Post-Treatment Follow-Up Visit

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIANS: Head and Neck Medical Oncology; Radiation Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Annual Surveillance — 12 months following completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation
REFERRING PROVIDER: Multidisciplinary Head and Neck Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 53-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, returning today for her 12-month post-treatment surveillance visit following definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue. She presents for annual clinical assessment, flexible laryngoscopic examination, review of interval imaging and ancillary testing, and ongoing discussion of late treatment effects and survivorship planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (Stage I per AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharynx classification), following evaluation of a 2.5 cm left level II cervical lymph node and a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass identified on CT neck and confirmed on PET-CT staging. Pathology confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong diffuse p16 positivity and positive high-risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node confirmed metastatic squamous carcinoma. NGS from the primary biopsy specimen demonstrated a PIK3CA E545K hotspot mutation, TRAF3 truncating mutation, EP300 and FBXW7 mutations, and KMT2D truncating mutation, with low tumor mutational burden (4 mut/Mb) and microsatellite stability. PD-L1 CPS was reported at 18 by external send-out.

She was evaluated at our multidisciplinary tumor board and treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation: IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk planning target volume, with elective nodal coverage per protocol. Cisplatin was administered at 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22; the third planned cycle on day 43 was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and concurrent grade 3 mucositis. She required hospitalization during week 4 of treatment for grade 3 oral mucositis with severe pain, complete inability to tolerate oral intake, and associated weight loss of 5 kg. A prophylactic PEG tube had been placed prior to the start of chemoradiation and was used for full nutritional support during the acute toxicity period.

She completed all 35 fractions of radiation without treatment breaks. Post-treatment PET-CT at approximately 10 weeks showed complete metabolic response at the primary site and at the involved left level IIA lymph node, with no new sites of disease. The PEG tube was removed at approximately 3 months post-treatment after successful transition to full oral intake.

At her 6-month surveillance visit and accompanying CT neck/chest, she continued to show no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease, with the left level IIA nodal region showing a small fibrotic remnant and no suspicious enhancement. She was noted at that visit to have grade 2 late radiation fibrosis of the left neck with reduced range of motion, and radiation-induced hypothyroidism with a TSH of 7.2, necessitating an upward adjustment in her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Physical therapy for cervical myofascial release and range-of-motion exercises was initiated.

She presents today for her 12-month comprehensive annual assessment. Since the last visit, she reports ongoing improvement in swallowing function, continued mild residual xerostomia, and improving neck range of motion with physical therapy. She remains employed full-time as a high school English teacher. She denies any new throat pain, odynophagia, hemoptysis, hoarseness, new neck masses, otalgia, or unintentional weight loss. No new neurologic symptoms, dyspnea, or constitutional complaints.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: She reports persistent mild dry mouth, manageable and somewhat improved from earlier in her recovery. She uses Biotene oral rinse and xylitol-containing lozenges with subjective benefit. She notes occasional mild dysphagia when eating hard, dry textures such as raw vegetables and crackers; softer and moist foods are generally well-tolerated without difficulty. She denies new throat pain, odynophagia to liquids, hemoptysis, hoarseness, or otalgia. No trismus. She notes mild bilateral submandibular region tightness consistent with ongoing xerostomia and mild fibrosis. No new neck masses detected by self-examination.

Musculoskeletal/Neck: Left neck stiffness is notably improved since initiation of physical therapy. Range of motion has increased significantly. She continues her home exercise program.

Endocrine: She denies fatigue, weight gain, constipation, or cold intolerance consistent with hypothyroidism. She reports consistent daily compliance with her current levothyroxine dose.

Cardiovascular/Pulmonary: Denies dyspnea, palpitations, cough, or chest discomfort.

Gastrointestinal: Appetite is fully restored. She denies reflux, nausea, or abdominal discomfort. GERD well-controlled on omeprazole (continued from prior to diagnosis).

Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or changes in urinary pattern. Renal function has recovered from cisplatin-associated nephrotoxicity as documented in prior notes.

Neurologic: No peripheral neuropathy, no cognitive complaints, no new cranial nerve deficits.

Psychological/Quality of Life: She describes her quality of life as good. She has returned to full-time work. She expresses some ongoing health anxiety around the possibility of recurrence, which she manages with mindfulness and peer support. She is interested in connecting with an HPV-positive head and neck cancer survivorship support community.

All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
- Levothyroxine 100 mcg orally once daily (increased from 75 mcg approximately 4 months ago for radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
- Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (pre-existing GERD)
- Biotene oral spray as needed for xerostomia
- Xylitol lozenges as needed
- Daily prescription-strength fluoride toothpaste (0.5% sodium fluoride) — dental oncology recommendation for radiation caries prevention
- Calcium carbonate 500 mg with Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily (recommended post-treatment for bone health)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (established, carried forward):
- HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0, Stage I (AJCC 8th ed.), diagnosed — treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin/IMRT, complete metabolic response confirmed
- Hypothyroidism (pre-existing, exacerbated by radiation — now on adjusted dose levothyroxine)
- Gastroesophageal reflux disease, mild, well-controlled on PPI
- Late radiation effects: grade 2 left neck fibrosis (improving on PT), grade 1 xerostomia, mild pharyngeal dysmotility (documented on MBS)
- Status post PEG tube placement (removed at ~3 months post-treatment)
- Cisplatin-associated nephrotoxicity, resolved (creatinine returned to baseline 1.0 mg/dL at prior visit)

SOCIAL HISTORY (carried forward):
- Never-smoker (0 pack-years)
- No significant alcohol use
- High school English teacher, currently employed full-time
- Of Nigerian-American descent
- Married, reports strong family support
- Physically active — has resumed walking and yoga

FAMILY HISTORY: No family history of head and neck cancers, no known hereditary cancer syndromes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. She is alert and interactive. She appears in excellent overall health compared to her clinical state at end of treatment approximately 12 months ago.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.4°F, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 67.8 kg (up from 63 kg at end-of-treatment nadir; approaching pre-treatment baseline of 68 kg).

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lesions.

Eyes: Conjunctivae clear, extraocular movements intact. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light.

Oral Cavity / Oropharynx: Oral mucosa is intact and well-healed. No ulceration or mucosal irregularity. Mild to moderate oral dryness with reduced salivary pooling consistent with ongoing xerostomia. Dentition intact with evidence of dental restoration work consistent with radiation precautions per dental oncology. No visible mucosal leukoplakia or suspicious lesion. Tongue mobile and symmetric. Buccal mucosa, floor of mouth, and hard and soft palate unremarkable on inspection.

Flexible Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy (performed in clinic today):
Nasopharynx: Normal mucosa bilaterally, no masses, Eustachian tube orifices patent.
Oropharynx: The base of tongue appears smooth and well-healed with no gross mucosal irregularity, ulceration, or exophytic lesion identified. There is mild generalized mucosal pallor and surface irregularity consistent with post-radiation change, but no suspicious nodularity or mass. Tonsils absent (lingual tonsil tissue present, appears atrophic consistent with radiation change). Posterior oropharyngeal wall smooth. Bilateral palatine tonsillar fossae without mass or ulceration.
Hypopharynx: Piriform sinuses symmetric and open. No mucosal lesions. Posterior hypopharyngeal wall normal.
Larynx: True vocal cords symmetric with normal mobility and full abduction. No lesion or irregularity. Supraglottis without mass. Subglottis not well-visualized but no concerning findings on scope assessment.
Overall: No gross evidence of recurrent disease on flexible laryngoscopy.

Neck: Soft, supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy in levels I through V bilaterally. Left neck demonstrates mild subcutaneous firmness/induration consistent with late radiation fibrosis, predominantly in the level II-III region; this appears stable and not appreciably worse than the prior visit. No discrete nodule or mass palpated. No right-sided abnormality. No midline or thyroid mass. Trachea midline.

Thyroid: Not enlarged, no palpable nodules.

Skin: Left cervical and submandibular skin shows mild hyperpigmentation and subtle textural change consistent with post-radiation dermatitis, now largely resolved with residual dyspigmentation. No wound breakdown or induration concerning for chondronecrosis.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Well-healed PEG tube site without surrounding erythema or granulation tissue.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Shoulder elevation symmetric (cranial nerve XI preserved).

Performance Status: ECOG 0.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained at today's visit):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^3/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 13.4 g/dL (normal)
- Platelets: 241 x10^3/uL (normal)
- Neutrophil count: 3.2 x10^3/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL; essentially at baseline, complete recovery from prior cisplatin-associated nephrotoxicity)
- eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Glucose: 88 mg/dL
- ALT: 22 U/L, AST: 19 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L
- Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (normal; previously required supplementation during cisplatin therapy)
- Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL
- Total protein: 7.1 g/dL; Albumin: 4.3 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal; well-controlled on current levothyroxine 100 mcg)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

A surveillance CT of the neck and chest with IV contrast was obtained at the 12-month mark as part of the planned post-treatment surveillance protocol. Results were reviewed today.

CT Neck/Chest (12-month surveillance, reviewed today):
The left base of tongue appears unremarkable without any focal mass, abnormal density, or destructive change at the primary tumor site. There is no evidence of local tumor regrowth. The left level IIA nodal region shows minimal residual fibrotic tissue that is stable and smaller in caliber compared to prior imaging; no pathologic enhancement or central necrosis identified. No suspicious lymphadenopathy identified in the bilateral cervical, retropharyngeal, or mediastinal regions. Mild radiation-related changes in the left submandibular gland region. Chest survey within the scan windows is without pulmonary nodules or masses. No osseous lesions. Overall, no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease.

---

SPEECH AND SWALLOWING ASSESSMENT:

The patient was seen by our speech-language pathology team for a modified barium swallow (MBS) study at today's 12-month visit (results available for review). The MBS demonstrated:
- Mildly reduced base-of-tongue retraction during the pharyngeal swallow phase
- Mild pharyngeal dysmotility without frank aspiration on any food/liquid consistency
- Trace penetration (above the level of the cords but not passing through) on thin liquids, adequately managed with a chin-tuck compensatory maneuver without aspiration
- Normal laryngeal elevation and closure
- No pooling in the piriform sinuses or valleculae on soft or pureed textures

Impression from SLP: Mild residual pharyngeal dysmotility consistent with post-radiation changes. No aspiration on any tested consistency with compensatory technique. Diet: regular with modifications (avoid hard, dry, crumbly foods). Continued swallowing exercises recommended.

DENTAL SURVEILLANCE:
Dental oncology follow-up note available in chart. No new caries identified at most recent dental visit 6 weeks ago. Daily fluoride treatment ongoing. No evidence of osteoradionecrosis of the mandible.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE AT 12 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

Ms. Okonkwo has reached the 12-month post-treatment milestone with no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of recurrent or residual disease. The current CT neck/chest shows no concerning findings, and her exam with flexible laryngoscopy is entirely reassuring. She achieved a complete metabolic response on her 10-week post-treatment PET-CT, and this durable response has been maintained on all subsequent imaging and clinical assessments.

Her tumor biology is highly favorable: HPV-positive, p16-positive, never-smoker, T2N1, complete metabolic response after definitive chemoradiation. Published series from RTOG 0129, TROG 12.01, and ORATOR trial cohorts consistently demonstrate that patients with her profile have 5-year disease-specific survival rates exceeding 85-90%. We discussed these data with the patient today in plain language, emphasizing that reaching 12 months without recurrence represents a meaningful prognostic milestone and that with each additional clear surveillance visit, her overall risk of relapse continues to decline.

Surveillance plan: We will continue clinical surveillance at 6-month intervals through year 3, then annually thereafter per current head and neck oncology guidelines. CT neck and chest will be obtained at 18 months post-treatment (planned). We do not intend to obtain PET-CT at this time given the complete and durable response, lack of any clinical concern for recurrence, and existing data that routine late PET-CT in complete responders carries a low diagnostic yield and potential for false positives. She should return to the emergency department or contact the clinic immediately if she develops a new neck mass, rapidly progressive dysphagia, hemoptysis, or other concerning symptoms between visits.

2. LATE RADIATION EFFECTS — XEROSTOMIA AND PHARYNGEAL DYSMOTILITY

Xerostomia is currently grade 1, tolerable, and managed with Biotene spray, xylitol lozenges, and generous hydration. She should continue these measures. There is no evidence that pilocarpine would add meaningful benefit at this stage given her current symptom burden; we will revisit if symptoms worsen. She was counseled to avoid medications with anticholinergic side effects and to maintain meticulous oral hydration.

Swallowing function has continued to improve and her MBS shows only mild residual dysmotility without aspiration. She will continue twice-weekly swallowing therapy with our SLP team with a structured home exercise program including Shaker exercises, Mendelsohn maneuver practice, and effortful swallow. Dietary modification (avoiding hard, dry, crumbly foods) is appropriate and does not significantly limit her quality of life. We do not anticipate need for PEG tube reinsertion based on her current trajectory.

Dental surveillance is ongoing with dental oncology. Daily prescription fluoride is essential given radiation dose to the mandible and salivary glands. She is doing well with this.

3. RADIATION-RELATED LEFT NECK FIBROSIS

Grade 2 late radiation fibrosis of the left neck noted at 8 months is improving with physical therapy. She reports significant improvement in range of motion and decreased stiffness. She will continue her physical therapy regimen and home stretching program. We will reassess at the next visit. No evidence of radiation-induced vascular injury; carotid duplex screening per institutional protocol has not yet reached the indicated timeframe (typically starting at 5 years post-radiation).

4. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM

TSH is now 2.1 on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, within the normal reference range. Thyroid function is well-controlled at this time. She will continue current dose with annual thyroid function monitoring. She was counseled on the expected natural history of radiation-induced hypothyroidism (often progressive) and the importance of continued monitoring. We will alert her primary care physician to continue annual TSH testing going forward.

5. RENAL FUNCTION — RESOLVED CISPLATIN NEPHROTOXICITY

Creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL, essentially at her pre-treatment baseline of 0.8 mg/dL. Renal function has fully recovered from the transient grade 2 nephrotoxicity during cisplatin therapy. Magnesium is now normal without supplementation. No specific renal monitoring beyond routine labs is needed at this time.

6. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

The patient expressed a strong interest in connecting with a survivorship program and HPV-positive head and neck cancer peer support group. We will provide referrals to our institutional survivorship clinic and to external resources including the Head and Neck Cancer Alliance and SPOHNC (Support for People with Oral and Head and Neck Cancer). A formal survivorship care plan summarizing her diagnosis, treatment, completed monitoring, and ongoing needs will be generated by our survivorship nurse coordinator and provided to her and her primary care physician. We discussed the importance of continued sun-protection, avoidance of any tobacco exposure, continued dental vigilance, and routine health maintenance.

We also addressed her health anxiety directly. Ongoing worry about recurrence is common and normal in head and neck cancer survivors, and we validated her experience. We recommended continued engagement with mind-body therapies such as yoga and mindfulness, which she has already incorporated into her routine. Formal psychological support or a referral to our oncology psychosocial support team is available if her anxiety worsens.

7. RETURN TO CARE

Ms. Okonkwo will return in approximately 6 months for her 18-month surveillance visit, at which time CT neck with contrast will be repeated per our protocol. She was provided with the oncology nurse navigator contact information for any interim concerns. Labs to be repeated at next visit include CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and TSH.

Time spent: 45 minutes total (examination, review of records, imaging discussion, counseling).

Electronic signature: Attending, Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Attending, Radiation Oncology
Cosigned by: Head and Neck Oncology Nurse Navigator
